Patch Set 1:

> why don't we refer the objects from osp_thandle instead?

I am not sure for that. But one side effect is that the object will be pinned in RAM (for sometime) before the callback done if we refer it directly. And if such object is marked as dying, then other visitors will be blocked until callback done. Such waiting may be another potential deadlock corner, although I have no detailed idea yet. 

Wangdi, do you have any idea about that? Thanks!

Patch Set 1:

I think the usual scenario is when the caller (creator?) of a transaction hold objects on its own.

Patch Set 1:

> I think the usual scenario is when the caller (creator?) of a transaction hold objects on its own.

That is possible, but that is not all the case, right?
Anyway, if we use the non-blocked method for osp_invalide(), even if the callback itself holds the reference, it is also not problem, right?

Patch Set 1:

the very obvious issue is an additional load on LU infrastructure - object lookup isn't free. actually I think the object must be pinned, this is how other backend works all the time and that should be fine.

Patch Set 1:

Current framework uses the "object_update" to store the "ou_fid", that is part of on wire data structure, it is not good idea to add pointer member in such structure, and enlarging  such structure will cause interoperability trouble.

On the other handle, we can enhance the object_find logic to make this case only to search the objects hash table, it is still non-free, but comparing with the network overhead, it is not expensive, especially consider that it will be done only under "failure" callback case.
Uploaded patch set 2.

Patch Set 2:

OSP needs to update objects in RPC reply, it's not just a failure case. and we put extra load on the _common_ LU infrastructure shared by the whole stack.
there is a structure holding callbacks for every update, probably we can put the pointers there.
Di?

Patch Set 2:

OSP needs to update objects in RPC reply, it's not just a failure case. and we put extra load on the _common_ LU infrastructure shared by the whole stack. there is a structure holding callbacks for every update, probably we can put the pointers there. Di?

Hmm, at least OSP does not update attribute and EA of the objects after reply, because the RPC is postponed to the transaction stop, but OSP has to update its attributes or EA in cache in the normal OSP_md_xxx(), i.e. these attributes (EA) will be updated before RPC.  You mean update sth else?

But in this case, it actually only invalidate object attributes for write object, so it probably make sense to link the write object into thandle instead of using lu_object_find() in the callback. Please correct me if I miss sth.

Patch Set 2:

I meant that we have a set of "read" methods which need to put data from RPC into the objects. and a single RPC may contain many of those.
can we reuse the structure osp_trans_callback() uses?

Patch Set 2:

I am fine either link these objects to RPC or transaction.

commit.subject: LU-8399 osp: direct reference object to be invalidate
commit.message:
LU-8399 osp: direct reference object to be invalidate

If the cross-MDTs update failed, the OSP transcation callback
will invalidate related object(s) cache. Originally, it uses
lu_object_find() to locate the object by FID. But it may be
blocked if related object is marked as dying (for some reason,
such as purged when umount).

To avoid such trouble, we make the "osp_update_request" to
reference related object(s) directly, then when needs to
invalidate, we can access the object(s) directly without
locating again.
